coredata.dc:title: Studies of photocatalytic and optoelectronic properties of microwave synthesized and polyethyleneimine stabilized carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ahlawat, Amit
Rana, Pawan S.
Solanki, Pratima R.
coredata.dc:description: The paucity of fresh drinking water is one of the chief issues of the twenty-first century. This has driven the advancement of various procedures for water refinement, for example, wastewater treatment. Organic coloring agents like Rhodamine-B (RhB) are quite possibly the foremost troublesome substances to manage. RhB is among the most significantly used coloring agents, which is hard to manage using conventional methodologies. Here, we report the photocatalytic degradation of RhB using microwave synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs). UV–vis absorption study and fluorescence study were used to confirm the formation of CQDs. The transmission electron microscope studies confirmed the small size of about 2 nm of spherical shaped CQDs. Degradation of RhB was studied under dark, under visible, and UV light irradiation. The highest degradation of 99.7% was obtained under UV- light irradiation in 150 min.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rhodamine-B
Carbon quantum dots
Polyethyleneimine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A (a) showing UV absorption spectra and photoluminescence spectra (PL) of CQDs, (b) showing the Tauc plot obtained from UV spectra, (c) left cuvette shows the CQDs under visible and right cuvette under UV light.

UV–vis absorbance of RhB under (a) dark, (b) visible light irradiation, (c) UV light irradiation, and (d) show the C/Co vs. time plot. The upper inset shows the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics for UV plot (c), while the lower inset shows the color change in the dye solution for (i) stock solution, (ii) after addition of H2O2 + acid, (iii) after 60 min of irradiation, (iv) after 150 min of irradiation.
Mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB using CQDs.
Comparison of present results with the previously reported in the literature.

head.ce:title: Studies of photocatalytic and optoelectronic properties of microwave synthesized and polyethyleneimine stabilized carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Special Center for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Special Center for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Deen Bandhu Chotu Ram University of Science and Technology, Murthal 131039, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The paucity of fresh drinking water is one of the chief issues of the twenty-first century. This has driven the advancement of various procedures for water refinement, for example, wastewater treatment. Organic coloring agents like Rhodamine-B (RhB) are quite possibly the foremost troublesome substances to manage. RhB is among the most significantly used coloring agents, which is hard to manage using conventional methodologies. Here, we report the photocatalytic degradation of RhB using microwave synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs). UV–vis absorption study and fluorescence study were used to confirm the formation of CQDs. The transmission electron microscope studies confirmed the small size of about 2 nm of spherical shaped CQDs. Degradation of RhB was studied under dark, under visible, and UV light irradiation. The highest degradation of 99.7% was obtained under UV- light irradiation in 150 min.

ce:section.ce:para: The scarcity of pure and freshwater is one of the significant problems of the 21st century [1]. The increase in the population has necessitated the adoption of water treatment to make wastewater reusable [2]. One of the major pollutants in wastewater is organic dyes such as Rhodamine-B (RhB) and Methylene Blue [3–5]. Among these, RhB is the most used dye in the coloring industry [6]. This is a cation dye with four ethyl groups attached to each end with high thermal and photostability [7,8]. This dye can cause severe harm to the health of human beings, such as dysfunction of kidneys, respiratory and intestinal infections, etc. [9–11]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess tunable optical properties by changing the size and ligand and high surface area to volume ratio, low cytotoxicity, protection from photobleaching, and biocompatibility. Therefore, CQDs have been explored for various applications [12–14]. The CQDs also possess excellent optoelectronic properties, as many authors have explained [15–16]. Among various other QDs (such as ZnS, MgS, VS2, etc.) [17–21], and semiconducting metal oxides (such as TiO2 nps, ZnO nps) [22–26], CQDs have not been explored for photocatalytic application. Most of the studies have involved the use of CQDs in composite form [27–30]. We have used CQDs for photocatalytic degradation of RhB. Also, the optoelectronic application of the CQDs was assessed using electrochemical measurement explained in detail in the Supplementary data provided along with the manuscript.

The synthesis of the CQDs was performed by modifying the microwave synthesis method, as reported in our previous paper [31]. CQDs were synthesized by taking 1 gm of citric acid in a beaker. Separately, a drop of polyethyleneimine was added into the beaker and thoroughly mixed. The beaker was kept in a microwave oven for 3 min, having 700 W of power to synthesize CQDs. Once the beaker was cooled, the solution was washed with deionized (DI) water and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm to remove the impurities and obtained CQDs. The optical properties of CQDs were studied using a UV–vis spectrometer (T90+, PG Instruments Ltd.). For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis, nanoparticles were dispersed in ethanol solution followed by ultrasonication to obtain the uniformly dispersed solution of CQDs, which were then spread on a carbon-coated copper microgrid and dried carefully. A 10-ppm RhB solution was prepared for the catalytic and photocatalytic study, ultrasonicated for 30 min, and further stirred at a constant rate of 250 rpm under dark to attain adsorption/desorption equilibrium. For the RHB dye degradation study was performed under dark (catalytic study), a small amount of nitric acid (HNO3 −0.1 M) was added to the solution to attain pH ∼ 2 as RhB is known to show good degradation under acidic conditions. After this few drops of stock hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were added to the CQDs. These samples were then studied in a closed chamber under dark, visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm using 100-Watt Philips bulb) and UV light (λ < 400 nm using 300-Watt Osram Lamp) irradiation. The distance of the bulbs from the beaker was 15 cm. Readings were taken at 0, 30, 60, 90, 120, and 150 min intervals.

The optical properties of CQDs were studied utilizing UV-absorbance and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 1 A shows the UV- absorbance and fluorescence spectra of CQDs. The left y-axis corresponds to the UV–Vis absorbance spectra, while the right y-axis corresponds to the fluorescence emission spectra. The UV–Vis absorption spectra peaks around 250 nm are due to π → π* transition, while the broad peak around 355 nm is due to n → π* transition in the CQDs. These peaks are usually centered on 280 nm and 310 nm. This shift in the position may be due to the use of polyethyleneimine in the synthesis procedure. The band gap was calculated using Tauc's plot and was found to be 3.2 eV (Inset, Fig.1A) [26]. On the other hand, high fluorescence emission was observed for the CQDs, with maximum emission obtained at 340 nm excitation. These results are in agreement with the previously reported literature [32–35]. Fig. 1 B (a) shows the transmission electron microscopy image of the CQDs. The CQDs are well distributed uniformly without aggregation. The excellent dispersion may be due to the polyethyleneimine. A histogram of these CQDs was plotted, and the average particle size was calculated found as 2.07 ± 0.02 nm which is similar to other literature reports [32–34].

Fig. 2 (a), (b), and (c) shows UV–vis absorption profiles of CQDs catalytic and photocatalytic performance over Rh-B performed under dark, visible, and UV light irradiation, respectively. Fig. 2(a) and (b) show no significant change in the UV absorption profile. While in Fig. 2(c), we can see a substantial decrease in the UV absorbance. The degradation percentage of dye under dark, visible, and UV light was 15, 20, and 99.7%, respectively. The pseudo-first-order kinetics was calculated for the UV light irradiated sample. Fig. 2(d) shows the C/Co and ln (Co/C) plot. The plot of ln (Co/C) was fitted with a linear function to assess the reaction rate constant (k) using the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model as described by Dhiman et al. [1]. The value of constant K was found to be 0.017 min−1.

Fig. 3 shows the mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB using CQDs. Since no shift was seen in the peak position, the mineralization process is responsible for the degradation of RhB. As explained previously by some authors [1,7,36], with the irradiation of light, electrons get excited and jump from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), generating holes in the VB. The electron on the surface of the CQDs gets transferred from the CB to the RhB molecule because of the difference in the electronegativity and reduction of O2 to O2 –. Simultaneously, holes in the VB change OH to OH
, causing oxidation. The O2 reacts with H2O to produce OH
. These OH
radicals further react with RhB molecules and convert them to H2O and CO2. This process is well known as the mineralization process.

Table 1 shows the comparison of CQDs with other QDs reported for photocatalytic degradation of RhB. This performance of CQDs confirms high photocatalytic activity against the degradation of RhB. Most of the studies on CQDs have been in the form of composite. They are used as a secondary material to improve the photocatalytic performance of the primary materials such as metal oxide (TiO2, CeO2, etc.). Here we have obtained good photocatalytic performance for CQDs without any other material.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been synthesized via microwave with citric acid and polyethyleneimine as precursors. A shift in the UV–Vis absorbance spectra has been seen with high fluorescence. Particle size was found to be around 2 nm calculated using TEM. The degradation percentage of dye under dark, visible, and UV light were 15, 20 and 99.7%, respectively. The highest degradation of 99.7% was obtained under UV- light irradiation in 150 min. The mineralization process was found to be a dominant process, and the first-order reaction rate constant was found to be 0.017 min−1. Electrochemical study was performed to assess the electronic properties of the material and CV and DPV studies were performed. In the electro-kinetics study, the heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant, surface concentration and diffusion coefficient values were found to be more for CQDs as compared to the ITO. These values imply that these CQDs can be used for biosensing purpose either using an enzyme or enzymeless.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the AIRF, JNU, for providing the TEM facility. One of the authors, Amit Ahlawat, is thankful to UGC, India, for giving financial support through fellowship (NTA Ref No. 201610066606). The Government of India provide financial supports for this work [Indo-Russia project (DBT/IC-2/Indo-Russia/2017-19/02)]. PSR also acknowledge to DST, India for DST-FIST project (Ref.No; SR/FST/PS-I/2012/32), sanctioned to Department of Physics, DCRUST, Murthal.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2021.130830.
